title: OS & Distributed Systems Lab Assignments
summary: Lab practicals covering OS fundamentals (CPU scheduling, memory placement, page replacement) in Java and distributed systems concepts (Lamport logical clocks, ring/bully election algorithms) in Python. Each module is a standalone lab exercise with no code dependencies between modules; all five groups can be implemented in parallel.
instructionPreview: This repository contains implementations of systems programming and distributed systems practicals from an undergraduate computer-engineering laboratory sequence. The code covers CPU scheduling algorithms, memory placement strategies, page replacement policies (Java), and distributed systems concepts including Lamport logical clocks and election algorithms (Python). Core algorithm implementations have been removed and must be re-implemented.
instruction:
This repository contains implementations of systems programming and distributed systems practicals from an undergraduate computer-engineering laboratory sequence. The code covers CPU scheduling algorithms, memory placement strategies, page replacement policies (Java), and distributed systems concepts including Lamport logical clocks and election algorithms (Python). Core algorithm implementations have been removed and must be re-implemented.

Your working directory is `/workspace`. The project code is already present.
You need to implement all the modules and features listed below so that
all tests pass.

## Modules to Implement

The following modules need to be implemented or substantially built out:

- **Lamport Clock** (`Distributed System/Clock Synchronization/lamport.py`): Logical clock implementation supporting event-driven clock increments. (~1 file, ~1 function to implement)

- **Election Algorithms** (`Distributed System/Election algorithm/ring_and_bully.py`): Ring election and Bully election algorithms for distributed coordinator selection. Nodes communicate to elect the node with the highest ID as coordinator. (~1 file, ~3 functions to implement)

- **CPU Scheduling** (`spos/B1_CPU Scheduling/`): Six CPU scheduling algorithm implementations — FCFS, SJF (preemptive), Non-preemptive SJF, Round Robin, Priority Scheduling (preemptive), and Non-preemptive Priority Scheduling. Each reads process details from stdin, computes completion time, turnaround time, and waiting time, then prints a scheduling table with averages. (~6 files, ~6 main methods to implement)

- **Memory Placement Strategies** (`spos/B2_Memory Placement Strategy/`): Four memory allocation policy implementations — First Fit, Best Fit, Worst Fit, and Next Fit. Each allocates processes to memory blocks according to its policy, tracks remaining block sizes, and prints an allocation table. (~4 files, ~4 functions to implement)

- **Page Replacement** (`spos/B3_Page Replacement/`): Three page replacement algorithm implementations — FIFO, Least Recently Used (LRU), and Optimal Page Replacement. Each simulates page frame management, tracks page hits and faults, and prints frame state snapshots with hit/fault ratios. (~3 files, ~3 main methods to implement)

## Test-Coupled Signatures

The following functions are called directly by the test suite. You must implement
them with these exact signatures:

- `LamportClock.tick(self) -> None` — increment the logical clock counter by 1
- `LamportClock.get_time(self) -> int` — return the current clock value
- `Node.initiate_ring_election(self, nodes: list) -> None` — initiate a ring election among the given nodes
- `Node.start_bully_election(self, nodes: list) -> None` — initiate a bully election among the given nodes
- `Node.send_bully_election_message(self) -> None` — handle receiving a bully election message
- `static void firstFit(int[] blockSize, int m, int[] processSize, int n, int[] remblockSize)` — first-fit memory allocation printing a table with "Not Allocated" for unfit processes
- `static void bestFit(int[] blockSize, int m, int[] processSize, int n, int[] remblockSize)` — best-fit memory allocation printing a table with "Not Allocated" for unfit processes
- `static void worstFit(int[] blockSize, int m, int[] processSize, int n, int[] remblockSize)` — worst-fit memory allocation printing a table with "Not Allocated" for unfit processes
- `static void NextFit(int[] blockSize, int m, int[] processSize, int n, int[] remblockSize)` — next-fit memory allocation printing a table with "Not Allocated" for unfit processes

moduleDag.description: Lab practicals covering OS fundamentals (CPU scheduling, memory placement, page replacement) in Java and distributed systems concepts (Lamport logical clocks, ring/bully election algorithms) in Python. Each module is a standalone lab exercise with no code dependencies between modules; all five groups can be implemented in parallel.

nodes.label: Lamport Logical Clock
nodes.description: Implements a Lamport logical clock with tick() (increment counter by 1) and get_time() (return current counter value). Supports event-driven clock increments for distributed system simulations.

nodes.label: Ring and Bully Election Algorithms
nodes.description: Implements distributed coordinator election via two algorithms: ring election (passes election token around a ring, highest ID wins) and bully election (higher-ID nodes bully lower ones until the highest-ID node claims coordinator).

nodes.label: CPU Scheduling Algorithms
nodes.description: Six Java programs implementing CPU scheduling policies: FCFS, preemptive SJF, non-preemptive SJF, preemptive priority, non-preemptive priority, and round-robin. Each reads process arrival/burst times from stdin and prints a completion/TAT/WT table.

nodes.label: Memory Placement Strategies
nodes.description: Four Java implementations of dynamic memory allocation policies: First Fit, Best Fit, Worst Fit, and Next Fit. Each allocates processes to memory blocks and prints a table showing block assignment and remaining block size, marking unfit processes as "Not Allocated".

nodes.label: Page Replacement Algorithms
nodes.description: Three Java programs simulating virtual memory page replacement: FIFO, LRU, and Optimal. Each manages a fixed-size page frame, tracks page hits and faults, and prints frame snapshots plus hit/fault ratios.
